text: Graham Wallas, in his 1926 work The Art of Thought, mapped the creative process into four stages: preparation, incubation, illumination, and verification. The incubation stage — often dismissed as idleness — is where unconscious cognition performs its most consequential work. Neuroimaging studies show that the default mode network, active during mind-wandering, integrates disparate memories and forges remote associations that focused attention suppresses. The paradox Wallas identified is that deliberate effort can obstruct the very synthesis it seeks. Stepping away from a problem is not abandoning it; it is delegating it to a cognitive system better suited to the task.
question: According to the passage, why does focused attention sometimes obstruct creative synthesis?

options.text: It suppresses the remote associations the default mode network would otherwise forge.
It exhausts working memory before connections can form.
It activates the default mode network prematurely.
It prevents the verification stage from completing.
